ENCODER_DECODER_START_DOCSTRING = r"""
    This class can be used to initialize a sequence-to-sequence model with any pretrained autoencoding model as the
    encoder and any pretrained autoregressive model as the decoder. The encoder is loaded via
    [`~TFAutoModel`] function and the decoder is loaded via [`~TFAutoModelForCausalLM`] function.
    Cross-attention layers are automatically added to the decoder and should be fine-tuned on a downstream generative
    task, like summarization.

    The effectiveness of initializing sequence-to-sequence models with pretrained checkpoints for sequence generation
    tasks was shown in [Leveraging Pre-trained Checkpoints for Sequence Generation
    Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.12461) by Sascha Rothe, Shashi Narayan, Aliaksei Severyn. Michael Matena, Yanqi
    Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu.

    After such an Encoder Decoder model has been trained/fine-tuned, it can be saved/loaded just like any other models
    (see the examples for more information).

    This model inherits from [`TFPreTrainedModel`]. Check the superclass documentation for the generic methods the
    library implements for all its model (such as downloading or saving, resizing the input embeddings, pruning heads
    etc.)

    This model is also a [tf.keras.Model](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/keras/Model) subclass. Use it
    as a regular TF 2.0 Keras Model and refer to the TF 2.0 documentation for all matter related to general usage and
    behavior.

    Parameters:
        config ([`EncoderDecoderConfig`]): Model configuration class with all the parameters of the model.
            Initializing with a config file does not load the weights associated with the model, only the
            configuration. Check out the [`~TFPreTrainedModel.from_pretrained`] method to load the model weights.
"""

# 🌐 Multilingual Text Classification

This project implements a multilingual text classification system using a pre-trained multilingual BERT model. The system is designed to classify text into one of 14 categories across multiple languages.

## 📋 Project Overview

The project consists of two main components:
1. **Training Pipeline** (`train.py`): Trains a multilingual BERT model for text classification
2. **Inference Pipeline** (`inference.py`): Uses the trained model to classify new text

## 🚀 Features

- Multilingual text classification (supports 14 languages)
- Pre-trained multilingual BERT model
- Easy-to-use inference interface
- Training pipeline with validation
- Support for custom datasets

## 📦 Installation

1. Clone the repository:
```bash
git clone <repository-url>
cd multilingual-text-classification
```

2. Install required dependencies:
```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## 🎯 Usage

### Training the Model

To train the model, use the `train.py` script:

```bash
python train.py --data_dir <path-to-data> --output_dir <path-to-save-model>
```

Arguments:
- `--data_dir`: Directory containing training data
- `--output_dir`: Directory to save the trained model
- `--epochs`: Number of training epochs (default: 3)
- `--batch_size`: Training batch size (default: 16)
- `--learning_rate`: Learning rate (default: 2e-5)

### Making Predictions

To classify text using the trained model:

```bash
python inference.py --model_path <path-to-model> --text "Your text here"
```

Arguments:
- `--model_path`: Path to the trained model
- `--text`: Text to classify
- `--lang`: Language code (optional, auto-detected if not provided)

## 📊 Dataset Format

The training data should be in CSV format with the following columns:
- `text`: The text to classify
- `label`: The category label (0-13)
- `language`: Language code (optional)

## 🏷️ Categories

The model classifies text into one of 14 categories:
0. World
1. Sports
2. Business
3. Sci/Tech
4. Health
5. Entertainment
6. Politics
7. Education
8. Environment
9. Culture
10. Travel
11. Food
12. Fashion
13. Lifestyle

## 📈 Performance

The model achieves competitive performance across multiple languages. Performance metrics are available in the training logs.

## 🤝 Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Please feel free to submit a Pull Request.

## 📄 License

This project is licensed under the MIT License.

## 🙏 Acknowledgments

- Multilingual BERT model from Hugging Face
- Dataset providers and contributors

s) <|file_sep|>README.md
# CodeBERT

This is the repository for the paper "[CodeBERT: A Pre-Trained Model for Programming and Natural Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.08155)".

CodeBERT is a pre-trained Transformer encoder for dual modalities, supporting both natural language (NL) and programming languages (PL). CodeBERT learns from the NL-PL pairs by leveraging the bimodal data of source code and its documentation. For example, the model can learn from a Python function and the corresponding docstring. We evaluate CodeBERT with downstream tasks including code search and code documentation generation. Across PLs like Python and Java, CodeBERT achieves SOTA performance in both tasks.

## News
- [2021/07/28] CodeBERT is accepted by TACL 2021!
- [2021/03/15] CodeBERT is available in Huggingface [transformers](https://huggingface.co/microsoft/codebert-base).
- [2020/10/05] CodeBERT is available in [Fairseq](https://github.com/pytorch/fairseq/tree/master/examples/codebert).
- [2020/09/14] CodeBERT is available in [ModelScope](https://modelscope.cn/models?name=codebert).
- [2020/04/02] CodeBERT is released.

## Pre-trained Model
We provide the pre-trained model in PyTorch and TensorFlow 2.0.

- [PyTorch](https://huggingface.co/microsoft/codebert-base)
- [TensorFlow 2.0](https://huggingface.co/microsoft/codebert-base-tf)

## Downstream Tasks
We evaluate CodeBERT with two downstream tasks: code search and documentation generation.

### Code Search
Code search aims to retrieve relevant code for a given natural language query. We evaluate CodeBERT on the [CodeSearchNet](https://github.com/github/CodeSearchNet) dataset.

Please refer to [codesearch](codesearch) for more details.

### Documentation Generation
Documentation generation aims to generate a natural language description for a given piece of code. We evaluate CodeBERT on the [CodeSearchNet](https://github.com/github/CodeSearchNet) dataset.

Please refer to [documentation](documentation) for more details.

## Citation
If you use this code, please consider citing:
```
@article{feng2020codebert,
  title={CodeBERT: A Pre-Trained Model for Programming and Natural Languages},
  author={Feng, Zhangyin and Guo, Daya and Tang, Duyu and Duan, Nan and Feng, Xiaocheng and Gong, Ming and Shou, Linjun and Qin, Bing and Liu, Ting and Jiang, Daxin and Zhou, Ming},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2002.08155},
  year={2020}
}
```
## Contact
If you have any question or comment about the code, feel free to contact Zhangyin Feng (zhangyin.feng@microsoft.com) or Daya Guo (daya.guo@microsoft.com).
